title: How has citizenship changed over time: level 2
topic_path: How has the definition of citizenship changed over time?
How has citizenship changed over time: level 2

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would violate the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A hospital administrator refuses to give a birth certificate to a child because their parents are undocumented.
A state passes a law that limits voting to white people as they were the original colonizers.
An election officer stops a woman from casting her vote in the presidential election.
A voting station only allows people with state-issued photo identification to vote in an election.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would violate the Fifteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An election officer stops a black man from entering a voting booth.
A state refuses to allow a white woman from registering to vote.
An election officer refuses to hand a ballot to an eighteen-year-old high school student.
A state limits voting to people who are not currently incarcerated.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would violate the Nineteenth Amendment?**	

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A state passes a law rescinding women’s right to vote in local and state elections.
An election officer refuses to give a ballot to a Mexican man because she thinks he’s undocumented.
A voting station removes a woman for wearing a “Vote for Kanye” shirt when she casts her vote.
A state closes several voting stations and replaces them with stations in predominantly white neighborhoods.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ingrid was born in St. Croix on the US Virgin Islands and has lived there all her life. She wants to vote in the Presidential primaries. 

**Can she vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Yes; she can vote in the primary elections, but she cannot vote in the general presidential election.
Yes; she can vote in both the primary election and the general presidential election.
No; she cannot vote in federal elections because she lives in a US territory.
No; she cannot vote in federal elections because she was not born in the United States.

assessment_items.question_content:
The year is 1875. A Black man named Clarence Meier wants to cast his vote in the presidential election. 

**Can he vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Yes; he can vote in the presidential election because of the Fifteenth Amendment.
Yes; he can vote because the Nineteenth Amendment protects his right to vote.
No; he cannot vote because the government does not consider him an American citizen.
No; he cannot vote because he does not have full voting rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
The year is 1840. A white woman named Julia Vinsot wants to cast her vote in the midterm elections.

 **Can she vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: No; she cannot vote because women did not get the right to vote until the ratification of the Nineteenth Amendment in 1920.
No; she cannot vote because the women did not consider women citizens until the ratification of the Fourteenth Amendment in 1870.
Yes; she can vote because white women inherited their citizenship from their husbands.
Yes; she can vote because she is voting in a midterm election, but not a presidential election.

assessment_items.question_content:
Valeria is a Hispanic, thirty-five-year-old woman. She cast a vote for the very first time in the 2018 midterm elections. 

**What amendments gave Valeria the right to vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Nineteenth Amendment
the Fifteenth Amendment
the Fourteenth Amendment
the Twenty-sixth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
DeAndre is a black, nineteen-year-old man. He casts a vote in the 2020 presidential election. 

**What amendments gave DeAndre the right to vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Fifteenth Amendment
The Twenty-sixth Amendment
The Fourteenth Amendment
The Nineteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
Abigail is an eighteen-year-old white woman. She just cast her vote in the 2022 midterm elections. 

**What amendments gave Abigail the right to vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Nineteenth Amendment
The Twenty-sixth Amendment
The Fourteenth Amendment
The Fifteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
Abdiel was born in San Juan, Puerto Rico. When he was learning about US history in school, he found out that he was an American citizen, not an immigrant as he had thought. 

**What piece of legislation made Abdiel a citizen?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Jones Act
the Dawes Act
the Snyder Act
the Fourteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
Malia was born on and has lived her entire life on the Navajo Nation reserve. She finds out from her parents she doesn’t have to apply for citizenship as she is already a citizen. 

**What piece of legislation made Malia a citizen?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Snyder Act
the Nineteenth Amendment
the Fourteenth Amendment
the Jones Act

assessment_items.question_content:
Walter was an enslaved person from the time he was born until the end of the Civil War. In 1868, he became a citizen of the United States.

**What piece of legislation made Walter a citizen?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Fourteenth Amendment
the Fifteenth Amendment
the Snyder Act
the Jones Act

assessment_items.question_content:
Sherman is a forty-five-year-old, indigenous man that lives on the Spokane Indian Reservation. He votes in the statewide election for senator.

 **What pieces of legislation gave Sherman the right to vote?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Snyder Act
The Fifteenth Amendment
The Twenty-sixth Amendment
The Jones Act

assessment_items.question_content:
Yahira is a sixty-three-year-old woman who lives in Aguadilla, Puerto Rico. She voted in the 2020 presidential primary for Bernie Sanders. 

**What pieces of legislation gave Yahira the right to vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Jones Act
The Nineteenth Amendment
The Fifteenth Amendment
The Snyder Act

assessment_items.question_content:
Wesley is a twenty-year-old Asian-American man who lives in Ocala, Florida. He plans to vote in the 2020 presidential election. 

**What pieces of legislation gives Wesley the right to vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Fifteenth Amendment
The Twenty-sixth Amendment
The Jones Act
The Snyder Act

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations is an example of an obligation that citizens in states have, but citizens living in territories do not have?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to pay federal income taxes
to register for selective service
to obey federal laws
to pay federal taxes like Social Security

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations is an example of a right that citizens in both states and territories have?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: right to vote in presidential primaries every four years
right to representation in Congress with voting rights
right to electoral votes in a presidential election
right to refuse to pay taxes for social services

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations is an example of a right that citizens in states have but American nationals in territories do not have?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: right to relocate within the United States without a passport
right to defend the country by serving in the military
right to vote in presidential primaries every four years
right to protect one’s inventions or intellectual property legally

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations is an example of a right that citizens in states have but citizens living in territories do not have?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: right to representation in Congress that can vote on legislation
right to petition the government and peaceably assemble
right to acquire, use, or dispose of property
right to move within the United States without a passport

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations is an example of a right that citizens in states have, but citizens living in territories do not have?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: right to vote in presidential elections every four years
right to hold elected office at within the government
right to a fair trial by a jury of one’s peers
right to organize a labor union for better working conditions

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations is an example of a right that citizens in states have but American nationals living in territories do not have?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: right to serve on a federal jury
right to enter lawful contracts
right to access public education
right to access Social Security
